audit_status.source_base: Greek Acts text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Ephesian disciples, holy Spirit, Tyrannus hall, unusual powers, sons of Sceva, costly scrolls, Artemis riot.

verses.restored: It happened while Apollos was in Corinth that Paul, passing through the upper regions, came to Ephesus and found some disciples.
He said to them, Did you receive holy Spirit when you trusted? They said to him, But we have not even heard whether there is holy Spirit.
He said, Into what then were you baptized? They said, Into John's baptism.
Paul said, John baptized a baptism of turning/realignment, saying to the people that they should trust into the one coming after him, that is, into Jesus.
Hearing this, they were baptized into the name of the Lord Jesus.
When Paul had laid hands on them, the holy Spirit came upon them, and they were speaking in tongues and prophesying.
There were about twelve men in all.
Entering the synagogue, he spoke boldly for three months, reasoning and persuading concerning the reign of the Divine.
But when some were hardened and disobedient, speaking harmfully of the Way before the multitude, he withdrew from them and separated the disciples, reasoning daily in the school of Tyrannus.
This happened for two years, so that all those dwelling in Asia heard the word of the Lord, both Judeans and Greeks.
The Divine was doing extraordinary powers through the hands of Paul,
so that even cloths or aprons from his skin were carried away to the weak, and the diseases left them, and harmful spirits went out.
Some of the traveling Judean exorcists attempted to name over those having harmful spirits the name of the Lord Jesus, saying, I adjure you by Jesus whom Paul proclaims.
There were seven sons of a certain Sceva, a Judean chief priest, doing this.
The harmful spirit answered and said to them, Jesus I know, and Paul I recognize; but you, who are you?
The human in whom the harmful spirit was leaped upon them, mastered them all, and prevailed against them, so that they fled out of that house naked and wounded.
This became known to all Judeans and Greeks dwelling in Ephesus, and fear fell upon them all, and the name of the Lord Jesus was magnified.
Many of those who had trusted were coming, confessing and reporting their practices.
Many of those who practiced magic arts brought together the scrolls and burned them before all; they counted their prices and found fifty thousand pieces of silver.
Thus by the Lord's power the word was growing and prevailing.
When these things were fulfilled, Paul set in the Spirit to pass through Macedonia and Achaia and go to Jerusalem, saying, After I have been there, it is necessary for me also to see Rome.
Sending into Macedonia two of those serving him, Timothy and Erastus, he himself stayed for a time in Asia.
About that time there happened no small disturbance concerning the Way.
For a certain man named Demetrius, a silversmith making silver shrines of Artemis, provided no little business to the craftsmen.
Gathering them and the workers of such things, he said, Men, you know that from this business comes our prosperity.
You see and hear that not only in Ephesus but almost in all Asia this Paul has persuaded and turned away a considerable crowd, saying that those made by hands are not gods.

Not only is there danger that this trade of ours may come into disrepute, but also that the temple of the great goddess Artemis be counted as nothing, and that she be about to be pulled down from her magnificence, whom all Asia and the inhabited world revere.
Hearing this and becoming full of rage, they cried out, saying, Great is Artemis of the Ephesians.
The city was filled with confusion, and they rushed with one accord into the theater, seizing Gaius and Aristarchus, Macedonians, Paul's travel companions.
Paul wanted to go in to the people, but the disciples would not let him.
Some of the Asiarchs also, being friendly to him, sent to him and urged him not to give himself into the theater.
Therefore some cried one thing, some another, for the assembly was confused, and most did not know why they had come together.
Some from the crowd instructed Alexander, the Judeans pushing him forward. Alexander motioned with his hand and wanted to make a defense to the people.
But recognizing that he was Judean, one voice came from all, crying for about two hours, Great is Artemis of the Ephesians.
The city clerk quieted the crowd and said, Men of Ephesus, who among humans does not know that the city of the Ephesians is temple-keeper of the great Artemis and of the heaven-fallen image?
Since these things cannot be denied, it is necessary for you to be quiet and do nothing reckless.
For you brought these men who are neither temple-robbers nor blasphemers of our goddess.
If therefore Demetrius and the craftsmen with him have a matter against anyone, courts are held and there are proconsuls; let them accuse one another.
But if you seek anything further, it will be resolved in the lawful assembly.
For indeed we are in danger of being accused of riot concerning today, since there is no cause by which we will be able to give account for this disorderly gathering.
Saying these things, he dismissed the assembly.
